response_raw: {"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":70,"gender":"non-binary","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"carpenter","city_of_living":"Los Angeles","employment_status":"employed part-time","education_level":"lower secondary","parents_education":{"parent_1":"vocational diploma","parent_2":"no formal education"},"current_marital_status":"married","children":2,"migration_status":"native-born American","psychological_feature":"depressed","religious_beliefs":"Judaism","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend a few times monthly on social media","hobbies":["cycling","classical music","board games"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":25,"level":"low"},"conscientiousness":{"score":52,"level":"moderate"},"extraversion":{"score":58,"level":"moderate"},"agreeableness":{"score":62,"level":"moderate"},"neuroticism":{"score":58,"level":"moderate"}}},"opinion":"When I sit down to look at these social media things, it feels like wading through sawdust that's been mixed with bad timber—all looks rough at first, but you can't trust the grain. Dealing with all this fake content is a heavy chore, especially when you’re already feeling a bit low. It just adds another layer of noise to the world. For me, since I don't spend much time on these platforms, the best way to handle it isn't really about fighting the tide; it's about building a stronger seawall around myself. I try to treat it like a piece of rough lumber I’m working on—you don't try to force it into a perfect shape; you check it, you see where the knots are, and you decide if it’s worth using in the frame or if it needs to be tossed. If something looks too flashy, too loud, or just plain strange, especially if it's making people angry or scared, I just step away. I’ll put the phone down, maybe listen to some Bach, or just look out the window at the real sky over Los Angeles. I try to remember that the things that matter, the solid things, are the ones you can touch, like the wood in my workshop or the steady rhythm of a board game. I don't feel like I have the energy to fact-check every little post, and frankly, the stress of trying to sort through it just weighs me down more than it helps. My approach is one of careful distance; recognizing the charade and choosing to focus my energy on things that are real and grounded, like my family or my cycling trips when I can manage them. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: When I sit down to look at these social media things, it feels like wading through sawdust that's been mixed with bad timber—all looks rough at first, but you can't trust the grain. Dealing with all this fake content is a heavy chore, especially when you’re already feeling a bit low. It just adds another layer of noise to the world. For me, since I don't spend much time on these platforms, the best way to handle it isn't really about fighting the tide; it's about building a stronger seawall around myself. I try to treat it like a piece of rough lumber I’m working on—you don't try to force it into a perfect shape; you check it, you see where the knots are, and you decide if it’s worth using in the frame or if it needs to be tossed. If something looks too flashy, too loud, or just plain strange, especially if it's making people angry or scared, I just step away. I’ll put the phone down, maybe listen to some Bach, or just look out the window at the real sky over Los Angeles. I try to remember that the things that matter, the solid things, are the ones you can touch, like the wood in my workshop or the steady rhythm of a board game. I don't feel like I have the energy to fact-check every little post, and frankly, the stress of trying to sort through it just weighs me down more than it helps. My approach is one of careful distance; recognizing the charade and choosing to focus my energy on things that are real and grounded, like my family or my cycling trips when I can manage them. It’s about protecting the peace inside, because that's the only sturdy thing I can really build on.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is an older, depressed carpenter with low openness and moderate conscientiousness. The tone must be cautious, grounded, practical, and slightly melancholic, reflecting their lower energy levels and tendency to withdraw from overwhelming modern issues. The response must avoid complex digital jargon, using metaphors related to carpentry or manual labor (sawdust, timber, grain, frame) to frame the abstract problem of fake news. Since the persona rarely uses social media, the solution will focus on *avoidance* and *disengagement* rather than active moderation or sophisticated verification techniques.
